architecture_style: Byzantine dome on pendentives; later minarets
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Former patriarchal cathedral, later mosque, now a museum-mosque complex famed for its immense central dome and marble revetment.
facts: Isidore of Miletus and Anthemius of Tralles were credited architects under Justinian.
Calligraphic roundels and mosaics from different periods coexist in the interior.
history: Built as the Great Church of Constantinople; Ottoman conquest added minarets and furnishings; modern Turkey alternated museum and prayer use.
significance: A hinge monument between Roman, Byzantine, and Ottoman histories.

architecture_style: Classical Ottoman mosque with cascade of domes
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Six-minaret imperial mosque facing the Hippodrome, named for İznik tiles that tint the prayer hall interior.
facts: Visitors should respect prayer times and modest dress at the entrance.
Courtyard arcades frame views toward Hagia Sophia across the park.
history: Commissioned by Ahmed I as an imperial foundation with külliye dependencies around the square.
significance: Sultanahmet's skyline pair with Hagia Sophia defines tourist Istanbul.

architecture_style: Medieval stone cylinder with conical roof
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Genoese watchtower turned observation deck over the Golden Horn. Queues peak at sunset for panoramic photos.
facts: The hill quarter around it is dense with cafés and music venues.
Elevator access leads to an upper gallery with safety barriers.
history: Part of Genoese Galata's walls; Ottoman and modern restorations adapted it for fire watch and tourism.
significance: The vertical marker between historic peninsula and Beyoğlu nightlife.

year_built: 15th c. core; vaulted streets expanded over centuries
architecture_style: Ottoman covered market streets and hans
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Labyrinth of jewellery, textile, and souvenir shops under brick vaults. Named gates open onto different neighbourhoods.
facts: Compare prices and enjoy tea offers as part of local retail etiquette.
Major holidays can shorten trader hours.
history: Mehmed II's charter shops grew into a walled commercial city-in-miniature between Beyazıt and the Golden Horn.
significance: Still a working wholesale and retail hub beneath the tourist surface.

license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Sultan's palace on the Seraglio Point with treasury, harem ticket sector, and terraces over the Golden Horn and Marmara.
facts: Separate tickets often apply to the Harem apartments.
Courtyard silence rules help preserve the museum atmosphere.
history: Primary residence until Dolmabahçe; collections of arms, porcelain, and relics accumulated across reigns.
significance: Essential context for Ottoman court life above the old harbour.

license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Public ferries link Europe and Asia with cheap decks for photographing yalı waterfront mansions, mosques, and bridges in sequence.
facts: İstanbulkart taps work on municipal ferries and trams.
Morning light from the Üsküdar line highlights the historic peninsula silhouette.
history: Maritime suburbs grew along steamboat routes; modern catamarans shorten peak-hour crossings.
significance: The calmest city tour is often a round trip on the water.

description: Forest of recycled Corinthian columns reflected in shallow water with modern walkways and lighting.
facts: Medusa column bases are reversed as spolia.
Concerts use the space for dramatic acoustics.
history: Stored water for the Great Palace quarter before Ottoman reuse and rediscovery.
significance: Underground counterpoint to Hagia Sophia's dome above.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.

description: Sinan's largest mosque complex with terraces overlooking Golden Horn ship traffic.
facts: Courtyard arcades frame views toward Galata.
Tombs of Süleyman and Hürrem sit in the garden.
history: Imperial foundation mosque crowning the city's third hill above the bazaars.
significance: Architectural peak of classical Ottoman Istanbul.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.

description: Crystal staircase and ceremonial halls where the empire's late sultans received diplomats.
facts: Guided interior tours prohibit casual photography in many rooms.
Clock museum wing sits beside the main gate.
history: Shifted court residence from Topkapı to European-style waterfront modernity.
significance: Bosphorus façade rival to Ortaköy mosques downstream.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.

description: Aromas of saffron, sumac, and lokum under brick domes near the New Mosque.
facts: Cash still helps for small spice sacks.
Ferry piers sit one block south toward Kadıköy.
history: Commissioned from Egyptian trade revenues; still mixes wholesale with tourist retail.
significance: Sensory anchor between Yeni Cami and Galata Bridge commuters.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.

description: Restored mosaic and fresco cycles narrate Mary's life in intimate side chapels.
facts: Check prayer-time closures when visiting as a museum.
Hilly walk from Golden Horn tram lines.
history: Theodore Metochites sponsored the Palaiologan redecoration before Ottoman conversion.
significance: Finest surviving Constantinopolitan narrative art outside the major museums.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.

description: Small tower restaurant on a Bosphorus islet reached by shuttle boats from both shores.
facts: Legends mix poisoned princess tales with ancient customs posts.
Night ferries frame sunset shots toward Hagia Sophia.
history: Byzantine chain anchor point became a lantern station and café landmark.
significance: Vertical punctuation visible from Üsküdar promenade.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
